Report Configuration

Function: nnp_build_cell_list_cache Module: cp2k.psmp Source: nnp_acsf.F:1982-2094 [...] Coverage (incl. loops): 0.08% | (excl. loops): 0.00% 
/home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F: 1982 - 2094
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


1982: !>        occupancy — independent of total system size.  No pbc() call in the inner loop: each image


[...]


1995:       INTEGER, INTENT(IN)                                :: i


1996: 


1997:       INTEGER                                            :: bx, by, bz, cell_idx, ind, img, j, &


1998:                                  j_type, n_count, s, k


1999:       INTEGER, DIMENSION(3)                              :: bin


2000:       REAL(KIND=dp)                                      :: cx, cy, cz, drx, dry, drz, max_cut_sq, &


2001:                                                             norm, r2


2002: 


2003:       ind = nnp%ele_ind(i)


2004:       max_cut_sq = nnp%max_cut*nnp%max_cut


2005: 


2006:       ASSOCIATE (cl => cell_list_cache)


2007:          ! Hoist centre coordinates as scalars (no array temporaries inside the bin walk)


2008:          cx = cl%coord_primary(1, i)


2009:          cy = cl%coord_primary(2, i)


2010:          cz = cl%coord_primary(3, i)


2011:          CALL nnp_cell_list_bin_from_position([cx, cy, cz], bin)


2012: 


2013:          DO bz = MAX(1, bin(3) - cl%bin_span(3)), MIN(cl%nbin(3), bin(3) + cl%bin_span(3))


2014:          DO by = MAX(1, bin(2) - cl%bin_span(2)), MIN(cl%nbin(2), bin(2) + cl%bin_span(2))


2015:          DO bx = MAX(1, bin(1) - cl%bin_span(1)), MIN(cl%nbin(1), bin(1) + cl%bin_span(1))


2016:             cell_idx = nnp_cell_list_linear_index(bx, by, bz)


2017:             img = cl%head(cell_idx)


2018:             DO WHILE (img > 0)


2019:                j = cl%image_atom(img)


2020:                ! Skip self-image (j == i AND shift == 0); other periodic images of i are valid.


2021:                IF (j == i .AND. cl%image_shift(1, img) == 0 .AND. &


2022:                    cl%image_shift(2, img) == 0 .AND. cl%image_shift(3, img) == 0) THEN


2023:                   img = cl%next(img)


2024:                   CYCLE


2025:                END IF


2026:                ! Manual scalar dr (avoid Fortran array temporaries)


2027:                drx = cx - (cl%coord_primary(1, j) + cl%image_translation(1, img))


2028:                dry = cy - (cl%coord_primary(2, j) + cl%image_translation(2, img))


2029:                drz = cz - (cl%coord_primary(3, j) + cl%image_translation(3, img))


2030:                r2 = drx*drx + dry*dry + drz*drz


2031:                ! Cheap reject before SQRT: anything past max_cut can't enter any group's list.


2032:                IF (r2 >= max_cut_sq) THEN


2033:                   img = cl%next(img)


2034:                   CYCLE


2035:                END IF


2036:                ! Hoist neighbour element type once (saves repeated indirect loads in the cmp).


2037:                j_type = nnp%ele_ind(j)


2038:                ! lazy sqrt: compute norm only if at least one group accepts this image


2039:                norm = -1.0_dp


2040:                ! Identical classification logic to the original double loop, scalar fill.


2041:                DO s = 1, nnp%rad(ind)%n_symfgrp


2042:                   IF (j_type == nnp%rad(ind)%symfgrp(s)%ele_ind(1)) THEN


2043:                      IF (r2 < nnp%rad(ind)%symfgrp(s)%cutoff_sq) THEN


2044:                         IF (norm < 0.0_dp) norm = SQRT(r2)


2045:                         n_count = neighbor%n_rad(s) + 1


2046:                         neighbor%n_rad(s) = n_count


2047:                         neighbor%ind_rad(n_count, s) = j


2048:                         neighbor%dist_rad(1, n_count, s) = drx


2049:                         neighbor%dist_rad(2, n_count, s) = dry


2050:                         neighbor%dist_rad(3, n_count, s) = drz


2051:                         neighbor%dist_rad(4, n_count, s) = norm


2052:                      END IF


2053:                   END IF


2054:                END DO


2055:                DO s = 1, nnp%ang(ind)%n_symfgrp


2056:                   IF (r2 < nnp%ang(ind)%symfgrp(s)%cutoff_sq) THEN


2057:                      IF (j_type == nnp%ang(ind)%symfgrp(s)%ele_ind(1)) THEN


2058:                         IF (norm < 0.0_dp) norm = SQRT(r2)


2059:                         n_count = neighbor%n_ang1(s) + 1


2060:                         neighbor%n_ang1(s) = n_count


2061:                         neighbor%ind_ang1(n_count, s) = j


2062:                         neighbor%dist_ang1(1, n_count, s) = drx


2063:                         neighbor%dist_ang1(2, n_count, s) = dry


2064:                         neighbor%dist_ang1(3, n_count, s) = drz


2065:                         neighbor%dist_ang1(4, n_count, s) = norm


2066:                      END IF


2067:                      IF (j_type == nnp%ang(ind)%symfgrp(s)%ele_ind(2)) THEN


2068:                         IF (norm < 0.0_dp) norm = SQRT(r2)


2069:                         n_count = neighbor%n_ang2(s) + 1


2070:                         neighbor%n_ang2(s) = n_count


2071:                         neighbor%ind_ang2(n_count, s) = j


2072:                         neighbor%dist_ang2(1, n_count, s) = drx


2073:                         neighbor%dist_ang2(2, n_count, s) = dry


2074:                         neighbor%dist_ang2(3, n_count, s) = drz


2075:                         neighbor%dist_ang2(4, n_count, s) = norm


2076:                      END IF


2077:                   END IF


2078:                END DO


2079:                img = cl%next(img)


2080:             END DO


2081:          END DO


2082:          END DO


2083:          END DO


2084:       END ASSOCIATE


2085: 


2086:    END SUBROUTINE nnp_compute_neighbors


2087: 


2088: ! **************************************************************************************************


2089: !> \brief Prepare (lazily rebuild) the module-level cell-list cache for this force evaluation.


2090: !>        Must be called once per force eval (from nnp_calc_energy_force) before the per-atom


2091: !>        nnp_compute_neighbors loop.  Cheap path is O(N): refresh PBC-wrapped coords and check the


2092: !>        Verlet skin.  Full rebuild (O(N*(2p+1)^3)) is triggered only when an atom has moved more


2093: !>        than 0.5*skin or when num_atoms / cell vectors / cutoff have changed.


2094: !> \param nnp ...

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 209.20 to 36.00 cycles (5.81x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 12% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


8% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 7% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 1% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 22% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 23% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Masked instructions


Detected masked instructions.

Details


Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your function is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.

Workaround


If your function is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your function. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

FMA


Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).



  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


17 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
2 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).


Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function


The binary function is composed of 25 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 15: addition or subtraction
  • 1: multiply
  • 9: divide
The binary function is loading 1372 bytes (171 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 1032 bytes (129 double precision FP elements).

Arithmetic intensity


Arithmetic intensity is 0.01 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Detected masked instructions: assuming all mask elements are active.
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 209.20 cycles. At this rate:

  • 5% of peak load performance is reached (6.56 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 7% of peak store performance is reached (4.93 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 209.20 to 168.25 cycles (1.24x speedup).

Source file and linesnnp_acsf.F:1982-2094

          
Modulecp2k.psmp

        

        
The function is defined in /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:1982,1995-2094.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 209.20 to 36.00 cycles (5.81x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 12% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


8% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 7% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 1% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 22% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 23% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Masked instructions


Detected masked instructions.

Details


Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your function is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.

Workaround


If your function is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your function. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

FMA


Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).



  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


17 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
2 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).


Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function


The binary function is composed of 25 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 15: addition or subtraction
  • 1: multiply
  • 9: divide
The binary function is loading 1372 bytes (171 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 1032 bytes (129 double precision FP elements).

Arithmetic intensity


Arithmetic intensity is 0.01 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Detected masked instructions: assuming all mask elements are active.
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 209.20 cycles. At this rate:

  • 5% of peak load performance is reached (6.56 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 7% of peak store performance is reached (4.93 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 209.20 to 168.25 cycles (1.24x speedup).
